paper_metadata.title: Public support for climate policies and its ideological predictors across countries of the Global North and Global South
paper_metadata.geographic_scope: Global North: Germany, UK, USA; Global South: Brazil, China, South Africa
paper_metadata.empirical_scope_or_sample: Online representative samples (quotas for age, gender, income); N=11,964 across six countries; no exclusions reported.
paper_metadata.carbon_tax_relevance_summary: Measures support for a “taxes” policy instrument composite including a carbon-emissions–based carbon border tax on imports, and estimates predictors of that support across six Global North/South countries.

determinants.definition: Pro-environment biospheric value orientation (e.g., protecting the environment/preserving nature).
determinants.mechanism: Stronger environmental values increase perceived moral legitimacy and necessity of carbon-emissions–based taxation.
determinants.exact_tax_policy_context: Support for the “taxes” climate policy instrument composite (includes a “carbon border tax” on imports based on carbon emissions).
determinants.outcome_variable: Policy support for taxes (tax policy instrument composite; 1–6 scale).
determinants.evidence_quote: “a positive relationship between biospheric values and policy support (B = 0.27–0.33 … p < .001)”
determinants.statistical_or_result_detail: B=0.27–0.33; 95% CI [0.25–0.31, 0.28–0.34], p<.001; association weaker for taxes than for regulatory/info instruments.
determinants.notes: Positive predictor for tax support; smaller magnitude for taxes than for other instruments.

determinants.definition: Individualistic worldview emphasizing personal autonomy and opposition to government telling people how to live.
determinants.mechanism: Individualistic worldviews reduce acceptance of state-led collective interventions such as carbon-emissions taxes.
determinants.exact_tax_policy_context: Support for the “taxes” composite (including carbon border tax).
determinants.evidence_quote: “a negative relationship between individualistic worldviews and policy support (B = − 0.23 to − 0.15 … p < .001)”
determinants.statistical_or_result_detail: B=−0.23 to −0.15; p<.001; negative association stronger for regulatory instruments and taxes vs subsidies/info.
determinants.notes: Clear negative predictor for tax support across countries.

determinants.definition: Trust in government/public authorities (composite across four public institutions).
determinants.mechanism: Higher trust increases confidence that carbon tax policy will be implemented in the public interest, raising support.
determinants.exact_tax_policy_context: Support for the “taxes” composite (includes carbon border tax).
determinants.evidence_quote: “a positive relationship between trust in public institutions and policy support (B = 0.11–0.20 … p < .001)”
determinants.statistical_or_result_detail: B=0.11–0.20; 95% CI [0.09–0.18, 0.12–0.22], p<.001; stronger association for regulatory instruments and taxes vs subsidies/info.
determinants.notes: Positive predictor for tax support; stronger for taxes than for subsidies/info.

determinants.definition: Self-assessed understanding of aspects/causes of climate change.
determinants.mechanism: More perceived knowledge increases perceived relevance and informed support for carbon-emissions–based taxes.
determinants.exact_tax_policy_context: Support for the “taxes” composite (including carbon border tax).
determinants.evidence_quote: “positive association between policy support and self-reported climate change knowledge (B = 0.08–0.12 … p < .001)”
determinants.notes: Positive association for tax support in reported models.

determinants.definition: Belief that preserving the free-market system is more important than localized environmental concerns.
determinants.mechanism: Free-market ideology conflicts with price-based/state-imposed carbon taxation, lowering acceptance.
determinants.exact_tax_policy_context: Support for the “taxes” composite (including carbon border tax).
determinants.evidence_quote: “negative relationship between policy support and free-market beliefs (B = − 0.46 to − 0.38 … p < .001)”
determinants.statistical_or_result_detail: B=−0.46 to −0.38; 95% CI [−0.49 to −0.41, −0.44 to −0.36], p<.001; negative association reported across instruments with strongest effects for regulation.
determinants.notes: Entered as an additional covariate in the hierarchical regression, so extracted here as a political control/predictor.

determinants.raw_name_in_paper: aggregated region (Global North versus Global South)
determinants.standardized_candidate_label: Global South vs Global North (tax support)
determinants.definition: Whether respondents are in Global South or Global North countries.
determinants.mechanism: North–South context shifts baseline acceptability of carbon-emissions–based tax proposals (e.g., carbon border tax).
determinants.exact_tax_policy_context: Aggregate mean support for the “taxes” policy instrument composite (includes carbon border tax).
determinants.evidence_quote: “Global Southern countries showed stronger support … for taxes (F(1, 11,962) = 1234.93, p < .001)”
determinants.statistical_or_result_detail: Region main effect on taxes support: F(1,11,962)=1234.93, p<.001, ηp²=0.09.
determinants.notes: Global South support for taxes is consistently higher.

determinants.definition: Interaction showing how the individualism–tax-support relationship differs between Global North and Global South.
determinants.mechanism: In Global North contexts, individualistic worldviews correspond more strongly to lower acceptance of carbon taxes.
determinants.exact_tax_policy_context: Interaction effects on policy support across policy types including the taxes composite.
determinants.outcome_variable: Policy support for taxes (within the modeled interaction).
determinants.evidence_quote: “individualistic worldviews were more strongly (and negatively) associated … in our Global Northern countries … (p < .001)”
determinants.statistical_or_result_detail: Stronger negative individualism association with policy support in Global North vs Global South across policy types incl. taxes (p<.001).

determinants.raw_name_in_paper: trust in public institutions × aggregated region
determinants.definition: Interaction indicating how the trust–tax-support relationship differs between Global North and Global South.
determinants.mechanism: Trust is a more important driver of acceptance of carbon tax proposals in Global North contexts.
determinants.exact_tax_policy_context: Interaction effects on policy support across policy types including the taxes composite.
determinants.outcome_variable: Policy support for taxes (within the modeled interaction).
determinants.evidence_quote: “trust in public institutions was more strongly (and positively) associated … in our Global Northern countries … (p < .001)”
determinants.statistical_or_result_detail: Stronger positive trust association with policy support in Global North vs Global South across policy types incl. taxes (p<.001).

determinants.standardized_candidate_label: Biospheric values × Global region (tax support moderation)
determinants.definition: Interaction indicating differential strength of the biospheric-values association with support for the taxes composite between Global North and Global South (exception for tax support).
determinants.mechanism: Regional/contextual factors condition how strongly pro-environment values translate into support for carbon-emissions–based taxation.
determinants.exact_tax_policy_context: Interaction in models of support for the “taxes” composite (includes carbon border tax).
determinants.evidence_quote: “…no interaction with biospheric values (with exception of tax support; see Tables 10–12…)”
determinants.notes: Main text does not specify which region has the stronger biospheric-values–tax-support slope; direction left as n.a.
